author: deme1744
content:
Yes, and thanks to these test vectors we already found and fixed some bugs. But more work to be done still.

I did just realize that the new release does have a bunch of test vectors that caused some false failures. Most of the added tests under `witness_validation_*` are supposed to fail stateless execution apparently. However because our tests are still only working with just the `executionWitness` and not yet the serialized `statelessInputBytes` and `statelessOutputBytes` our tests could not know. But the description made that clear.

So for now I've just quickly added the deserialization of `statelessOutputBytes` to know the result there and skip witness comparison if it is `successful_validation == false`.
This is not yet sufficient for a few tests that do have `successful_validation == true` but simply have some added unneeded witness data. So I'll have to be a bit more lenient when comparing these.

But I'll rework this soon to fully deserialize the Input also, compare it with `executionWitness` and also execute the given data (as opposed the only the one our implementation generates now).

As mentioned, the way I'm using these test vectors is to both validate our stateless execution implementation and our witness generation.

author: jsign
content: Pefect, that makes sense! Thanks for the update 🙏 Glad the tests helped surface bugs!

author: deme1744
content:
Regarding this test: `blockchain_tests/for_amsterdam/amsterdam/eip8025_optional_proofs/witness_bytecodes_contract_creation/witness_codes_create_same_hash_then_read.json` 
Do you have more context regarding the occurrence of such scenario? I kind of see this as an optimization, and I did implement it, but I have not merged it yet because I want to understand better if adding this complexity is worth it.

See https://github.com/status-im/nimbus-eth1/pull/4099 for more info.

author: jsign
content:
Ah, glad you touched on this case. Actually it had its own PR  https://github.com/ethereum/execution-specs/pull/2479
From the spec perspective, it was only one line change to support this. In the PR description I mention a futher optimization taht was intentionally not done since that felt like more agressive in the spec.
But maybe this one line change in the spec might be somewhat complex for Nimbus?

author: jsign
content: I don't have a strong opinion, but I think the main reason why this was done it is because it was a one-line-spec-change optimization so didn't feel too agressive.

author: jsign
content: I don't expect this case to happen organically that often -- so the only reason I included this witness optimization (there aren't that many) is because of 1-line-cost change in the spec.
